content.full_text:
California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 16, 1976


Thomas I. McKnew, Jr.
Assistant General Attorney
Atcheson, Topeka & Santa Fe
Railway Company
121 East Sixth Street
Los Angeles, CA 90014

Dear Mr. McKnew:

    This letter will supplement my letter of September 15,
1976. It has come to my attention that you are concerned
about the implications of allowing employees who contribute
to Civic Trust 80 to designate which candidates or parties
should receive their contributions. A similar question
recently was asked by an attorney representing another
political action committee and I am enclosing a copy of our
response to that attorney. I trust it will resolve your
questions.

    If you have any further questions, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Kenneth Finney
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

KF:plb
Enclosure

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

December 15, 1970

Thomas I. McKnew, Jr.
Assistant General Attorney
Atcheson, Topeka & Santa Fe
Railway Company
121 East Sixth Street
Los Angeles, CA 90014

Dear Mr. McKnew:

You have asked a number of questions based on the fact
that the Santa Fe Employee's Good Government fund (registered with
federal authorities as Civic Trust 80) plans to make contributions
to candidates for state and local office in California. You have
pointed out that the chairman of the Trust is in Washington, D.C.,
and that the treasurer of the Trust, and all of the Trust's records,
are in Chicago, Illinois. In addition, the Trust's primary cam-
paign activities involve candidates for federal office, not contri-
butions to state and local candidates in California.

Before answering your specific questions, I think it will
be helpful to outline briefly the status of Civic Trust 80 under
the Political Reform Act. Gov. Code Section 81001 et seq.
Under the Act, a committee which receives $500 or more in contri-
butions in a calendar year is a Section 82013(a) committee. How-
ever, pursuant to regulations adopted by the Commission, there are
two different types of Section 82013(a) committees. For purposes
of discussion I will refer to these two types of committees as
"single-purpose committees" and "multipurpose committees." A primary
purpose committee is a committee formed or existing primarily for
"political purposes;" and political purposes means existing for the
purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of the
voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or
candidates or the qualification or passage of any measure in the
state of California. Gov. Code Section 82015(a).
On
the other hand, a multipurpose committee is an entity which exists
for a variety of purposes, one of which is to make contributions or
expenditures in connection with California elections. The staff
has determined that Civic Trust 80 is a multipurpose committee
because most of its contributions and expenditures involve candi-
dates outside the state of California.

1/ All statutory references will be to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

Thomas J. Hickney, Esq.
September 13, 1976
Page Two

Since the Trust is a multipurpose committee, only pay-
ments received by it which are "earmarked" for making contributions
or expenditures are reportable contributions within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 12213(d).
A payment is "earmarked" when, at the time of making the payment,
the donor knows or has reason to know that the payment, or funds
with which the payment will be comingled, will be used to make
contributions or expenditures relative to California elections. If
civic Trust 80 begins to make contributions to California candidates,
the employees who contribute to the Trust will have reason to know
that their payments, or a portion thereof, will be used to make
contributions to California. Accordingly, a portion of these pay-
ments will be "contributions" received by Civic Trust 80.

A reliable method for determining what portion of a pay-
ment from an employee is a "contribution" is to allocate on the
basis of the amount of money contributed by Civic Trust 80 to Cali-
fornia candidates. For example, if the Trust receives $100,000 in
employee contributions and contributes $10,000 to California candi-
dates, it will have received $10,000 in "earmarked contributions";
and ten percent of each employee contribution will be a "contribution"
within the meaning of the Political Reform Act.2/ Of course, under
this system, once Civic Trust 80 has contributed $500 or more to
California candidates, it will have received $500 in "con-
tributions" and, therefore, will be a committee within the meaning
of Section 82013(a). Civic Trust 80 qualifies as a Section
82013(a) committee, it will be obligated to file a statement of
Organization and the required campaign statements pursuant to Section
84200, et seq.

With the foregoing as a frame of reference, it is possible
to answer your specific questions. The numbering of the following
responses parallels the numbering in your letter.

1. With respect to in-kind contributions in the form of
administrative support, overhead and salaries of personnel
administering the Trust, it is possible that a portion of
these in-kind contributions may be includable in determining
whether the Trust is a Section 82013(a) committee. However, a
number of caveats should be mentioned. First, only additional

2/ It the Trust does not know exactly how much it will
receive in employee contributions or how much it will contribute to
California candidates, good faith estimates are acceptable for
purposes of grossing.

Thomas I. McKnew, Jr.
September 15, 1976
Page Three

administrative and overhead costs incurred by the corporation
as a result of its support for the Trust will constitute an in-
kind "contribution" to the Trust. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18423(c)(3)(A). Moreover, only the prorated portion of the
salary of an employee who spends ten percent or more of his
compensated time during a calendar month engaging in support
activity for the Trust, or other political activities, will be
an in-kind contribution to the Trust. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18423. If, despite these limitations, the corporation
makes in-kind contributions to the Trust by reason of the
administrative support, these in-kind contributions should be
counted in determining whether the Trust has received $500 in
contributions. However, in determining the total amount of
the in-kind contributions that are attributable to California
elections, the Trust can prorate based on a comparison of its
contributions to California candidates to the total amount of
contributions received by the Trust. Only that prorated por-
tion of the in-kind contributions is "earmarked" for the mak-
ing of contributions and expenditures relative to California
elections.

2. The relevant consideration with respect to contri-
butions from employees is not the residence of the employee-
donor but whether the amount of contributions received by the Trust
which are "contributions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act is $500 or more. In determining the amount of
"contributions" received, the important consideration will be
whether the Trust has contributed $500 or more to California
candidates. If the Trust has contributed $500 or more to
California candidates, then it is readily apparent that at
least $500 of the payments received from its employees are
attributable to California activity and are, therefore, "con-
tributions." Consequently, the Trust would be a committee
under Section 82013(a).

3. The answer to your third question is essentially the
same as the answer to the second question; whether the Trust
has made $500 in contributions to California candidates is
the relevant criterion. The important question is whether
the Trust has received $500 in "contributions." If the Trust
has contributed $500 or more to California candidates, then
a petitioner has received $500 in contributions and will be a
Section 82013(a) committee.

4. The Trust must report the amount and source of each
contribution for which this information is required regardless
of the residence of the donor. The essential factor is whether
a donor has contributed $50 or more relative to California

Thomas I. McNew,
September 15, 1975
Page Four

elections. If he or she has, then that donor must be dis-
closed on the campaign statement. However, in determining
whether any particular donor has contributed $50, it is appro-
priate to prorate according to the method previously described.
Thus, if the Trust receives a total of $100,000 from the cor-
poration's employees and makes contributions to California
candidates of $15,000, ten percent of the funds received by
the Trust are "contributions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act. Thus, ten percent of the total amount contributed
by each donor to the Trust counts toward determining whether
that particular donor has made a contribution of $50 or more
relative to a California election. Under these hypothetical
facts the Trust must identify each person who donated a total
of $500 or more to the Trust.

5. The requirement that the name, address, occupation
and employer of a donor be disclosed applies only to donors
who have made reportable contributions of $50 or more.

6. On the first page of Form 420, which requires the
disclosure of each candidate and ballot measure to which a
contribution has been made, only the California candidates
supported by the Trust should be listed. However, on Schedule
5 of Form 420, it is permissible either to list all recipients
of all contributions by the Trust or merely to list only the
California recipients, whichever you prefer.

7. Establishing a subsidiary of the Trust in California
would not change the reporting obligations which have been
outlined above. This is because the Trust still would be
receiving payments which are "earmarked" for the making of
contributions in California (the lump sum contribution to the
subsidiary). Since the Trust still would be receiving "ear-
marked" contributions, it would become a committee when it
received $500 in such contributions. Moreover, to determine
when this threshold was reached, the procedure would be the
same as that outlined above; and the Trust's reporting re-
quirements with respect to the questions you have posed also
would be the same. Of course, the subsidiary also would be a
Section 2013(a) committee once it received $500 in contribu-
tions and it too, therefore, would have to file a Statement of
Organization and campaign statements.

3/ Because the subsidiary would be making contributions
only to California candidates, it would be a primary purpose com-
mittee and, therefore, all payments it received and all expendi-
tures it made would be reportable contributions and expenditures.
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215(b) and 18225(b).

Thomas I. McNow,
September 15, 1976
Page Five

In conclusion, I want to say that the questions you have
posed present some complex issues and I encourage you to contact
the staff for further assistance in resolving your problems and for
clarification of the matters discussed in this letter. I also want
to mention that in respect to allocating employee contributions
in order to determine the amount which is a "contribution" under
the Political Reform Act, a system other than that which I have
outlined would be acceptable if it represents a good faith effort
to reflect the Trust's activities in California. I apologize for
the delay in responding to your letter.

Sincerely,

Kenneth Finney
Chief
Legal Division

RP:plh

The Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway Company
A Santa Fe Industries Company
121 East Sixth Street, Los Angeles, California 90014, Telephone 213/628-0111

CALIFORNIA LAW DEPARTMENT                    File 126-6L(1)


July 27, 1976


Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
State of California
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

     Re:  Request for Advice Under FPPC Regulation Adopted
          May 4, 1976, as Section 18329, California
          Administrative Code

     This company, the corporate offices of which are located in
Chicago, Illinois, provides administrative support for a voluntary Santa Fe Employees Good Government Fund registered with federal authorities as "Civic Trust 80."

     Voluntary contributions are made to the fund by management
employees, who elect to do so, in some 20 states in which we
operate, including California. The primary purpose of the fund
is to provide financial campaign assistance for candidates of
both major parties for Congressional office, although from time-
to-time prior to 1972 contributions from the fund were also made
to state and local candidates.

     The Chairman of the Board of Trustees of Civic Trust 80, a
Santa Fe employee, is based in Washington, D.C. The Treasurer,
also a Santa Fe employee, is located in Chicago, where records
of receipts and expenditures are maintained and reports required
by federal and/or state law are prepared.

Daniel H. Lowenstein
July 27, 1976
Page -2-

No contributions have been made from the fund to state or
local candidates within California since the passage of the
Political Reform Act of 1974.

The Trustees of Civic Trust 80 are now considering the possibility of making contributions to state and local candidates
in California in connection with the November General Election,
and are therefore attempting to ascertain their reporting responsibilities, and the mechanics thereof, under the Political
Reform Act. The questions which arise in this respect flow from
the facts that the fund is not domiciled in California; that
distributions to the fund are received from donors in multiple
states; and that contributions from the fund may be made to candidates for state and local office in several states, as well as
to candidates for federal office. Civic Trust 80 may therefore
become a "committee" under the definition contained in Section
82013 of the Political Reform Act.

Given the above circumstances, I shall appreciate your consideration of the following questions:

1. The threshold requirement for a "committee" under
   Section 82013(a) of the Act is reached when "contributions" received total $500 or more in a calendar year. In this context, are in-kind contributions by this corporation to Civic Trust 80 in
   the form of administrative support, overhead, and
   salaries of personnel administering the fund included in arriving at the $500 threshold?

2. Would Civic Trust 80 be deemed a "committee" under Section 82013(a) if contributions received from
   California employee donors total less than $500
   but the sum of contributions from all donors is
   more than $500?

3. If contributions received by Civic Trust 80
   from California employee donors are less than $500,
   but the total of all contributions received is

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
July 27, 1976
Page -3-

more than $500, and contributions to candidates
and committees under Section 82013(c) are less
than $5,000, is Civic Trust 80 a "committee"
for the purposes of the Act?

4. Must Civic Trust 80 report the amount and source
of each contribution received regardless of the
residence of the donor, or should the reporting
of contributions received be confined to those
donors residing in California?

5. Must the reporting of contributions include the
name, address, occupation, and name of employer
only for donors of $50 or more, or for all donors
regardless of amount?

6. Should all contributions from the fund to can-
didates for federal office and for state or lo-
cal office in any state be reported, or should
reporting of contributions be confined to those
made to candidates for state and local office
within California?

(The above questions stem partially from the mechanics of
reporting on Committee Campaign Statement Form 420, which call
for the reporting of all monetary contributions to the fund and
payments from the fund together with remaining surplus or de-
ficit; also to be considered is the fact that contributions from
the fund to California state and local candidates may or may not
exceed the amounts contributed to the fund by California donors.
In your reply, I would appreciate if you would describe the exact
reporting required in terms of the information to be elicited on
Form 420.)

7. Although answers to the above questions are never-
theless desired, it may be that the reporting dif-
ficulties incident to a fund domiciled outside
California may be obviated by the establishment

Daniel H. Lowenstein
July 27, 1976
Page -4-

and registration within California of a fund sub-
sidiary to Civic Trust 80. If this were done, it
is anticipated that a lump-sum contribution from
Civic Trust 80 would be made to the California Com-
mittee, from which contributions would in turn be
made to California state and local candidates. Ap-
propriate reports would then be rendered by the
subsidiary fund to the California Secretary of
State, listing the lump sum contribution received
and contributions to candidates made therefrom.
The lump sum contribution from the parent fund to
the California subsidiary fund might or might not
exceed $5,000, and as explained above, the primary
source of the lump sum contribution would be the
contributions of employees within and outside
California. In what circumstances would Civic
Trust 80, domiciled in Chicago, incur reporting
obligations under the California statute, and what
specific reporting would be required?

I shall be grateful for your early response.

Very truly yours,


Thomas I. McKnew, Jr.
Asst. General Attorney

TIM:pq
